event-brief:quebec-settlement:brief.spoken: In 1534–1535 Jacques Cartier sailed up the St. Lawrence, visited Stadacona (today's Québec City), and claimed the valley for France as "Canada." France's early colonization was disrupted by religious wars at home until the late 1500s. For our tree, this French doorway leads to the Mary Ann Goodwater branch.

event-brief:quebec-settlement:standard.spoken: In 1534, France's King Francis I authorized navigator Jacques Cartier, a French-Breton maritime explorer, to lead a voyage to the New World to seek gold and other riches, as well as a new sea route to Asia. In 1535, Jacques Cartier and his crew sailed up the St. Lawrence River and visited an Iroquois settlement of about 500 persons called "Stadacona" on the bluff where Québec City stands today. Cartier named the river the St. Lawrence. While in the New World he claimed the large St. Lawrence River valley for France, calling the area "Canada", after the Huron-Iroquois word "kanata". Cartier's later expeditions up the St. Lawrence and the building of Fort Charlesbourg-Royal enabled France to lay claim to the lands that would become the French colony of Québec. Between 1562 and 1598, France's colonization efforts in North America were disrupted by religious wars at home between French Roman Catholics and the rising French Protestant Huguenots. In 1578, eighty-six years after Columbus reached the Americas, England began its own efforts to explore and settle the New World. The English monarchy and early Parliament had been distracted by wars in Scotland, Ireland, and France, and by Protestant–Catholic conflict at home — leaving little energy for the New World until later in the 1600s. For the Winifred Coss Family Tree, this is the doorway to the Goodwater branch: French settlers and farmers whose descendants eventually meet the Powers branch in Iowa in 1853.

event-brief:quebec-settlement:deep.spoken: In 1534, France's King Francis I authorized navigator Jacques Cartier to explore the New World. Cartier's 1535 voyage up the St. Lawrence brought him to Stadacona — an Iroquois settlement on the bluff where Québec City stands — and he claimed the river valley for France, naming the country "Canada" from the Huron-Iroquois word "kanata." Fort Charlesbourg-Royal and later expeditions let France hold its claim to what became the colony of Québec. Meanwhile, between 1562 and 1598, France's colonization was repeatedly interrupted by wars between Roman Catholics and Huguenot Protestants at home — the same era of religious upheaval that shapes the English chapters of this story. England's own sustained New World push came later: by 1578 the crown was still absorbed in Scotland, Ireland, France, and domestic Protestant–Catholic conflict. The two colonial stories run in parallel for generations before they meet in one family. For the Winifred Coss Family Tree, Québec is not background colour — it is the origin of the Mary Ann Goodwater branch. French farmers and settlers along the St. Lawrence are direct ancestors on that line. Their descendants carry the name Goodwater forward until, in 1853, Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Ann Goodwater and the Goodwater and Powers branches join.

event-brief:salem-witch-trials:brief.spoken: In 1691, Martha (Barrett) Sparks of Chelmsford was accused of witchcraft and sent to a Boston prison on 28 October, while her husband Henry Sparks was away fighting in King Philip's War. She was released on 8 December 1692 after fifty-eight weeks — a direct family tie on the Powers branch through the Barrett line.

event-brief:salem-witch-trials:standard.spoken: Leading context: In the late 1500s European Christians developed a theory that witches made a pact with the devil and worked in groups. King James VI of Scotland (later James I of England) fueled a witch panic from 1590 and wrote Daemonologie in 1597. In Massachusetts, Salem was settled in the 1620s–1630s. The Barrett family arrived with the Winthrop Fleet and moved to Chelmsford in 1663. The colony executed its first accused witch, Margaret Jones, in 1648. In October 1691 King William and Queen Mary rechartered the colony as the Province of Massachusetts Bay under Governor Sir William Phips — while Puritans feared losing control. What witchcraft meant: Before 1692 it was a capital crime but hard to prove; accusation alone could ruin someone because prisoners paid their own jail costs. In spring 1692, while Phips was away, courts allowed spectral evidence and created a Court of Oyer and Terminer that assumed guilt until innocence was proved. The family event: Martha (Barrett) Sparks of Chelmsford was imprisoned on 28 October 1691. Boston jailer John Arnold also held famous Salem prisoners. Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned for Martha's release on 1 November 1692; she was freed on 8 December 1692 after fifty-eight weeks. Lagging family line: Rebecca Barrett married Walter Powers III in 1696; their son Josiah Powers Sr. was born in 1710 on Neshoba Hill — the Edith Powers branch of Winifred's tree.

event-brief:salem-witch-trials:deep.spoken: Leading — ideas: In the late 1500s a Christian theory held that witches made deliberate pacts with the devil and did not act alone. In 1590, while James VI was king of Scotland, more than a hundred people were accused of plotting his death by witchcraft — including charges that witches caused a storm that sank one of his ships. James published Daemonologie in 1597. Between 1603 and 1625 Scotland held about twenty witchcraft trials involving nearly 450 suspects, with roughly half executed. Leading — colony: Puritans founded Salem in 1626–1630. Thomas Barrett and Margaret (Huntington) arrived about 1635, moved to Chelmsford in 1663, and their granddaughter Martha (Barrett) Sparks lived thirty miles west of Salem. In 1648 Massachusetts executed Margaret Jones, a Charlestown midwife — the first of about eighty New England witch accusations before 1693, with fifteen executions. Leading — 1691 government: On 7 October 1691 William and Mary rechartered Massachusetts as the Province of Massachusetts Bay and sent Sir William Phips as royal governor. The charter tightened English control and alarmed Puritans. What is "Witchcraft"? Dad's document answers this directly. In the 1600s English American colonies, witchcraft before 1692 meant: a capital crime that was difficult to prove; formal accusation alone could punish someone, because prisoners paid all their own jail expenses whether guilty or innocent; cases went through the regular court system with ordinary rules of evidence; if found innocent, the accused could sue the accuser for slander or defamation to recover costs; and many accusations were common curses not meant literally — like calling someone a bad name. What changed in 1692: In spring 1692, while Governor Phips was away handling Native issues on the western frontier, the rules changed and convictions became easier. Witchcraft was still a capital crime, but courts now allowed "spectral evidence" — testimony that the accused person's spirit appeared in a dream while their body was elsewhere. A special Court of Oyer and Terminer (French for "to hear and to determine") assumed guilt until innocence was proved. Accused witches were shackled, denied food, and pressured to confess through intimidation, lack of legal counsel, and torture. The accused still paid all court costs; if found guilty they forfeited all property. Event — Martha Sparks: On 28 October 1691 Martha (Barrett) Sparks, daughter of Thomas Barrett Jr., was sent to a Boston prison on suspicion of witchcraft while her husband Henry Sparks fought in King Philip's War. She was never brought to trial. Jailer John Arnold held her for fifty-eight weeks alongside Sarah Good, Sarah Osbourn, and Rebecca Nurse from Salem. Event — wider panic: Governor Phips left immediately for the western frontier, leaving a leadership vacuum. Ralph Farnham Jr. died in Andover in January 1692 amid the Mary Tyler trial; Ralph Farnham III and John Farnham were summoned in the Martha Carrier trial — Carrier was hanged 19 August 1692 after smallpox devastated her family. Resolution: On 1 November 1692 Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned Phips (Massachusetts Archives Vol. 135 No. 64), citing twelve months and five days in prison with no evidence and no accuser, while he cared for Martha's two children and an ill wife. On 6 December 1692 he guaranteed a bond; Martha was released 8 December 1692. Dad's document points to the Jessie Maud Paff family story for more witch-trial history. Lagging — Powers branch: Rebecca Barrett (born 1673, Chelmsford) married Walter Powers III on 16 December 1696 in Littleton. Their son Josiah Powers Sr. was born 22 September 1710 in a Garrison House on Neshoba Hill — carrying the line to Edith Powers and eventually Winifred Eloise Coss.

chapter:seg-01-doors-opening:brief.spoken: The story does not begin with our people. It begins with the doors they would later walk through — kingdoms arguing over God, kings needing money, and a cold river in a country no Englishman yet called home.

chapter:seg-01-doors-opening:standard.spoken: The story does not begin with our people. It begins with the doors they would later walk through — kingdoms arguing over God, kings needing money, and a cold river in a country no Englishman yet called home. In 1485, on a Leicestershire field at Bosworth, the last Plantagenet king fell and a Welsh-born Tudor lifted a crown from the mud. Henry VII meant to end the Wars of the Roses; what he really did was hand his teenage son a restless island that would spend the next century tearing at its own faith. That son became Henry VIII in 1509 — eighteen years old, gifted, vain, and hungry for a male heir.

chapter:seg-01-doors-opening:deep.spoken: The story does not begin with our people. It begins with the doors they would later walk through — kingdoms arguing over God, kings needing money, and a cold river in a country no Englishman yet called home. In 1485, on a Leicestershire field at Bosworth, the last Plantagenet king fell and a Welsh-born Tudor lifted a crown from the mud. Henry VII meant to end the Wars of the Roses; what he really did was hand his teenage son a restless island that would spend the next century tearing at its own faith. That son became Henry VIII in 1509 — eighteen years old, gifted, vain, and hungry for a male heir. England was still Catholic then, still Latin in its liturgy, still paying its conscience to Rome. Within a generation all of that would crack. Across the Channel in 1517, a German monk named Martin Luther hammered his objections to indulgences onto a church door in Wittenberg. He had read the Latin Bible until the words burned in his hands. Rome ordered his books burned in 1521; by then the argument could not be unlit.

chapter:seg-02-two-doorways:brief.spoken: In 1575, while Elizabeth's court still balanced Catholic memory against Protestant law, a boy was born in Paris who would outlive every throne in this chapter. Louis Hébert — nine July 1575 — was not yet Canada's first farmer; he was simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells, in a kingdom that had its own wars of religion still to fight.

chapter:seg-02-two-doorways:standard.spoken: In 1575, while Elizabeth's court still balanced Catholic memory against Protestant law, a boy was born in Paris who would outlive every throne in this chapter. Louis Hébert — nine July 1575 — was not yet Canada's first farmer; he was simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells, in a kingdom that had its own wars of religion still to fight. Six years later and a hundred leagues north across the sea, another birth quietly mattered as much. On 25 March 1581, George Alcock came into the world at Cambridge in Cambridgeshire, son of Protestant minister John Richard Alcock and Alice Emily Abrich. No crown noted the day.

chapter:seg-02-two-doorways:deep.spoken: In 1575, while Elizabeth's court still balanced Catholic memory against Protestant law, a boy was born in Paris who would outlive every throne in this chapter. Louis Hébert — nine July 1575 — was not yet Canada's first farmer; he was simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells, in a kingdom that had its own wars of religion still to fight. Six years later and a hundred leagues north across the sea, another birth quietly mattered as much. On 25 March 1581, George Alcock came into the world at Cambridge in Cambridgeshire, son of Protestant minister John Richard Alcock and Alice Emily Abrich. No crown noted the day. No patent was issued. But the Powers branch of this tree — the English line that would one day meet the Hébert line in Iowa — starts here, in a university town smelling of river mud and ink. France was filling the St. Lawrence with names our cousins would carry. In Perche, Abraham Côsté was born in 1576; in La Rochelle, Jean Galleran Martin — called the Merchant of Metz — would father children who crossed the Atlantic.

chapter:seg-03-atlantic-crossings:brief.spoken: The Atlantic in these years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, compasses that lied in fog, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares.

chapter:seg-03-atlantic-crossings:standa.spoken: The Atlantic in these years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, compasses that lied in fog, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares. Our people were still mostly in parish registers on both shores, but the water between them was filling with names that would matter. In England the births came steady as rain. William Preston in Chesham in 1591.

chapter:seg-03-atlantic-crossings:deep.spoken: The Atlantic in these years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, compasses that lied in fog, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares. Our people were still mostly in parish registers on both shores, but the water between them was filling with names that would matter. In England the births came steady as rain. William Preston in Chesham in 1591. Edmund Rice in Berkhamsted in 1594. John Upham in Devon, John Bent in Hampshire, William Towne in Norfolk, Martha Blanchard and Arthur Gary — each a line on a page that would later cross the sea. On 6 January 1609, Thomas Barrett was born at Thornbury in Gloucestershire to Christopher Barrett and Elizabeth Clarke; his granddaughter would one day sit in a Boston jail accused of witchcraft. On 8 July 1605, Walter Powers Sr. was baptized at Stanton St.

chapter:seg-04-planting-towns:brief.spoken: After 1625 the story stops circling the Atlantic and starts putting down roots — wooden towns on Massachusetts mudflats, stone chimneys above the St. Lawrence ice, meetinghouses where freemen argued and rivers where beaver pelts bought muskets.

chapter:seg-04-planting-towns:standard.spoken: After 1625 the story stops circling the Atlantic and starts putting down roots — wooden towns on Massachusetts mudflats, stone chimneys above the St. Lawrence ice, meetinghouses where freemen argued and rivers where beaver pelts bought muskets. Two branches of one tree planted in the same generation, unaware of the Iowa wedding waiting at the far end. In 1626, while failed Gloucester fishermen retreated to Naumkeag and renamed it Salem without the king's permission, registers on both shores kept recording names. On 1 January, John Douglas Alcock was born at Markfield to George Alcock and Anne Hooker.

chapter:seg-04-planting-towns:deep.spoken: After 1625 the story stops circling the Atlantic and starts putting down roots — wooden towns on Massachusetts mudflats, stone chimneys above the St. Lawrence ice, meetinghouses where freemen argued and rivers where beaver pelts bought muskets. Two branches of one tree planted in the same generation, unaware of the Iowa wedding waiting at the far end. In 1626, while failed Gloucester fishermen retreated to Naumkeag and renamed it Salem without the king's permission, registers on both shores kept recording names. On 1 January, John Douglas Alcock was born at Markfield to George Alcock and Anne Hooker. Deacon John Upham married Elizabeth Webb at Bicton on 1 November. Robert Smith was born at Boston, Lincolnshire; Solomon Johnson married Eleanor Crofts in London. In France: Françoise Morin at Gahard, Anne Cloutier at Mortagne on 19 June, Pierre Tremblay at Randonnai, Pierre Saint-Denis and Vivienne Brunel at Dieppe and Rouen, Etienne Dumay Sr. at Dieppe, Sébastienne Veillon near La Rochelle, Marguerite Couillard at Québec on 6 August to Guillaume Couillard and Guillemette Hébert. Champlain granted Louis Hébert more land along the Saint-Charles River and opened the farm country at Château-Richer across from Île d'Orléans.

chapter:seg-05-frontier-and-faith:brief.spoken: After 1655 the margins grew teeth. English towns pushed inland toward groves already cleared by Pennacook hands; French farms clung to riverbanks while Iroquois warriors watched from every cedar swamp.

chapter:seg-05-frontier-and-faith:standa.spoken: After 1655 the margins grew teeth. English towns pushed inland toward groves already cleared by Pennacook hands; French farms clung to riverbanks while Iroquois warriors watched from every cedar swamp. Faith meant different things on each shore — Puritan meetinghouses where Eliot's Praying Indians wore English coats, and stone chapels where Jesuits exorcised devils from teenage girls. Both branches were about to learn that frontier peace is always borrowed time. 1656–1658 kept the registers busy on both sides of the ocean.

chapter:seg-05-frontier-and-faith:deep.spoken: After 1655 the margins grew teeth. English towns pushed inland toward groves already cleared by Pennacook hands; French farms clung to riverbanks while Iroquois warriors watched from every cedar swamp. Faith meant different things on each shore — Puritan meetinghouses where Eliot's Praying Indians wore English coats, and stone chapels where Jesuits exorcised devils from teenage girls. Both branches were about to learn that frontier peace is always borrowed time. 1656–1658 kept the registers busy on both sides of the ocean. Edward Peter Hurst died in Boston; his daughter Margaret had married Edward Preston at New Haven in 1651. William Towne sold Salem land and bought Topsfield meadow. Robert Smith, tailor of Rowley, married Mary French. Eleven-year-old William Taylor came alone on the Speedwell toward country that would become Marlborough. In Canada, Robert Drouin's prosperity showed in dowries — from nothing in 1656 to milk cows, clothing, and livres cash within eight years.

chapter:seg-06-wars-and-witch-panic:brie.spoken: The war did not end when the smoke cleared. It changed shape — from burning garrisons to burning charters, from Iroquois canoes on the Richelieu to English cannon on the St.

chapter:seg-06-wars-and-witch-panic:stan.spoken: The war did not end when the smoke cleared. It changed shape — from burning garrisons to burning charters, from Iroquois canoes on the Richelieu to English cannon on the St. Lawrence, from frontier militia to courtroom spectral evidence. Between 1676 and 1692 both branches learned that peace treaties and prayer books are written on paper the powerful can rewrite overnight. King Philip's War bled out through 1676.

chapter:seg-06-wars-and-witch-panic:deep.spoken: The war did not end when the smoke cleared. It changed shape — from burning garrisons to burning charters, from Iroquois canoes on the Richelieu to English cannon on the St. Lawrence, from frontier militia to courtroom spectral evidence. Between 1676 and 1692 both branches learned that peace treaties and prayer books are written on paper the powerful can rewrite overnight. King Philip's War bled out through 1676. At the Great Swamp Fight sixty-eight colonists died and a hundred fifty were wounded; twenty-two more perished on the march home. Phineas Upham, carried wounded to Wickford, then dragged through the "Hungry March" where soldiers ate their own horses — died of his wounds at Malden, date unknown but autumn 1676. Groton burned 13 March; Richard Sawtelle fled thirty miles east to Watertown with his family, leaving one of five garrison houses in ashes. Widower William Taylor married widow Hannah Merriam 16 July 1677 — her first husband Henry Axtell killed on the Marlborough-Sudbury road in April 1676; she brought five children. Eliezer Taylor born April 1678 was named for Deacon William Ward's son slain in the war.

chapter:seg-07-british-north-america:bri.spoken: After the witch panic, the colonies did not rest. They multiplied — towns west of Boston, farms on stolen Praying land, stone forts on the Richelieu, trading posts on lakes no European map had named.

chapter:seg-07-british-north-america:sta.spoken: After the witch panic, the colonies did not rest. They multiplied — towns west of Boston, farms on stolen Praying land, stone forts on the Richelieu, trading posts on lakes no European map had named. Between 1693 and 1750 the English branch learned to live as subjects of a crown, not shareholders in a company; the French branch learned that peace treaties with thirty-nine nations could hold sixteen years and still leave the border smoking. One million English souls against sixty thousand French by mid-century — yet Canada covered more ground than the thirteen colonies, and every river between them was a future battlefield. The Powers line took root where Nashoba had been.

chapter:seg-07-british-north-america:dee.spoken: After the witch panic, the colonies did not rest. They multiplied — towns west of Boston, farms on stolen Praying land, stone forts on the Richelieu, trading posts on lakes no European map had named. Between 1693 and 1750 the English branch learned to live as subjects of a crown, not shareholders in a company; the French branch learned that peace treaties with thirty-nine nations could hold sixteen years and still leave the border smoking. One million English souls against sixty thousand French by mid-century — yet Canada covered more ground than the thirteen colonies, and every river between them was a future battlefield. The Powers line took root where Nashoba had been. 16 December 1696, Walter Powers III married Rebecca Barrett at Littleton — granddaughter of Chelmsford Barretts, daughter of Joseph and Martha Gould — and kept an inn on the northerly part of town near Brown Hill and Spectacle Pond. When elder brother William died, Walter inherited their father Walter Jr.'s Concord house; by 1704 he was caring for sixty-five-year-old Walter Jr. and sixty-three-year-old Tryall at Nashoba. 22 September 1710, Josiah Powers Sr. was born in a garrison house on Neshoba Hill — seventh and last child of Walter and Rebecca, the name that would reach Vermont and Iowa.

chapter:seg-08-empires-at-war:brief.spoken: The final French and Indian War was not a footnote — it was the hinge. Sixty thousand French subjects against two million British colonists, yet Canada held the interior until the ships and muskets tipped.

chapter:seg-08-empires-at-war:standard.spoken: The final French and Indian War was not a footnote — it was the hinge. Sixty thousand French subjects against two million British colonists, yet Canada held the interior until the ships and muskets tipped. When the smoke cleared in 1763, New France was British, thirteen colonies were angry, and two branches of one family stood on opposite sides of a border that would not hold peace for twenty years. Between 1751 and 1815 the English line learned revolution; the French line learned to live under a Protestant king and keep their language; and a Cree métis girl on the Red River would one day meet a Vermont soldier's grandson in a way no parish register in Perche could have predicted. 28 May 1754: twenty-two-year-old Lt.

chapter:seg-08-empires-at-war:deep.spoken: The final French and Indian War was not a footnote — it was the hinge. Sixty thousand French subjects against two million British colonists, yet Canada held the interior until the ships and muskets tipped. When the smoke cleared in 1763, New France was British, thirteen colonies were angry, and two branches of one family stood on opposite sides of a border that would not hold peace for twenty years. Between 1751 and 1815 the English line learned revolution; the French line learned to live under a Protestant king and keep their language; and a Cree métis girl on the Red River would one day meet a Vermont soldier's grandson in a way no parish register in Perche could have predicted. 28 May 1754: twenty-two-year-old Lt. Colonel George Washington ambushed a French patrol at Jumonville Glen — the Forks of the Ohio, Fort Duquesne, Pittsburgh not yet invented. The fourth French and Indian War exploded across the continent and the Atlantic; Britain blockaded Canada while Acadians who refused the oath were scattered — some toward Louisiana, seed of the Cajun name. Seven Years' War followed in Europe 1756. Montcalm took Fort William Henry 1757; Native allies massacred surrendered British troops and carried smallpox back to the Ohio valley. Louisbourg fell 1758.

chapter:seg-09-west-to-iowa:brief.spoken: Peace did not mean stillness. The Treaty of Ghent had ended the shooting, but the continent kept rearranging itself — borders surveyed straight because rivers were too quarrelsome, fur companies merged because competition bled profit, and young people who had never seen Perche or Cambridge started walking toward land no parish had named.

chapter:seg-09-west-to-iowa:standard.spoken: Peace did not mean stillness. The Treaty of Ghent had ended the shooting, but the continent kept rearranging itself — borders surveyed straight because rivers were too quarrelsome, fur companies merged because competition bled profit, and young people who had never seen Perche or Cambridge started walking toward land no parish had named. Between 1816 and 1853 the English branch learned canals and railroad timetables; the French branch learned to cross borders without papers and change a surname to English; and Iowa, which had been someone else's cornfield yesterday, became the place where Edith Powers' grandson would marry a métis girl's daughter and finally stitch Winifred's tree. 20 October 1818: the United States and Britain signed the Convention of 1818 — fisheries, slave restoration clauses, joint occupation of Oregon, and a boundary at the 49th parallel because a straight line surveys easier than a watershed. Britain ceded Rupert's Land south of the line, including the Red River Colony below the parallel; the United States gave up the northern edge of Missouri Territory.

chapter:seg-09-west-to-iowa:deep.spoken: Peace did not mean stillness. The Treaty of Ghent had ended the shooting, but the continent kept rearranging itself — borders surveyed straight because rivers were too quarrelsome, fur companies merged because competition bled profit, and young people who had never seen Perche or Cambridge started walking toward land no parish had named. Between 1816 and 1853 the English branch learned canals and railroad timetables; the French branch learned to cross borders without papers and change a surname to English; and Iowa, which had been someone else's cornfield yesterday, became the place where Edith Powers' grandson would marry a métis girl's daughter and finally stitch Winifred's tree. 20 October 1818: the United States and Britain signed the Convention of 1818 — fisheries, slave restoration clauses, joint occupation of Oregon, and a boundary at the 49th parallel because a straight line surveys easier than a watershed. Britain ceded Rupert's Land south of the line, including the Red River Colony below the parallel; the United States gave up the northern edge of Missouri Territory. French missionaries at Red River worked with Ojibwe and Cree families; by 1823 roughly eight hundred métis households lived in the colony and present-day North Dakota. In 1821 Parliament forced the North West Company into the Hudson's Bay Company — André Carrière's employer changed names but not latitude. 1820 census: Nathaniel Powers at Westminster, Vermont, with Esther and seven children including twenty-year-old Edith; widow Elizabeth Johnson Smith at Phillipston; Warren Coss, twenty-nine, in Lowell but missing from the enumerator's book. 30 September 1824, Warren Coss married Edith Powers at Lowell, Middlesex County — the short Coss stub grafted onto the long Powers limb Nathaniel and Esther had grown in Vermont hill towns. July 1827, Joseph Warren Coss was born to Warren and Edith; no birth certificate survives and census pages disagree between Massachusetts and New Hampshire, but Martha Coss, one year old, died at Peterborough two months later — the grave argues for Hillsborough County.

chapter:seg-10-dakota-years:brief.spoken: The wedding in Winneshiek County was not an ending — it was a departure. Between 1854 and 1928 the merged family learned what it meant to hold land with a filing fee and a prayer, to lose a wife at thirty-nine on a Red River floodplain, and to raise children within earshot of tepees and buffalo hunts.

chapter:seg-10-dakota-years:standard.spoken: The wedding in Winneshiek County was not an ending — it was a departure. Between 1854 and 1928 the merged family learned what it meant to hold land with a filing fee and a prayer, to lose a wife at thirty-nine on a Red River floodplain, and to raise children within earshot of tepees and buffalo hunts. Homestead patents, Civil War pensions, stagecoach miles, and a hotel ledger at Grand Forks carried the story from Iowa corn to Dakota wheat while cousins in Ontario fished Lake Huron and a métis great-grandfather's name still appeared on a St. Boniface map. 1854 poured settlers into Iowa — ferries over the Mississippi running day and night, canvas tents blooming on open ground.

chapter:seg-10-dakota-years:deep.spoken: The wedding in Winneshiek County was not an ending — it was a departure. Between 1854 and 1928 the merged family learned what it meant to hold land with a filing fee and a prayer, to lose a wife at thirty-nine on a Red River floodplain, and to raise children within earshot of tepees and buffalo hunts. Homestead patents, Civil War pensions, stagecoach miles, and a hotel ledger at Grand Forks carried the story from Iowa corn to Dakota wheat while cousins in Ontario fished Lake Huron and a métis great-grandfather's name still appeared on a St. Boniface map. 1854 poured settlers into Iowa — ferries over the Mississippi running day and night, canvas tents blooming on open ground. 29 March, Benjamin Goodwater III naturalized at Winneshiek and homesteaded Frankville, twenty miles from Minnesota; Québec abolished the seigneurial system he had already left behind. Henry Raymond and Elizabeth Falconer married at Saugeen, Bruce County, Ontario — no register found. 8 November 1855, Edgar Putnam Coss was born at Sauk Centre, Wisconsin, two hundred sixty miles northwest of his parents' Iowa wedding. 1860 census: Warren Coss machinist at Hebron, Jefferson County, with Edith, twins Helen and Henry, Whitcomb, and young Goodwater nephews in the house; Joseph and Mary Ann farmed Cherry Grove, Minnesota, beside Norman Goodwater; Benjamin held twelve hundred dollars of Iowa soil at Glenwood. Minnesota statehood 1858 left unorganized land the Sioux had not surrendered; Congress created Dakota Territory 2 March 1861.

chapter:seg-11-depression-and-dust:brief.spoken: 24 October 1929, Wall Street broke. North Dakota, already a farm state on the knife edge of weather, fell twice — prices for wheat and cattle collapsed while drought turned the western half of the country to powder.

chapter:seg-11-depression-and-dust:stand.spoken: 24 October 1929, Wall Street broke. North Dakota, already a farm state on the knife edge of weather, fell twice — prices for wheat and cattle collapsed while drought turned the western half of the country to powder. 1934 and 1936 were the cruelest years: grasses failed, cows starved, scorching winds piled dirt like snowdrifts, and grasshopper clouds ate what the wind left. More than a hundred twenty-one thousand people left the state; twelve thousand three hundred eight farms were foreclosed. Winifred Eloise Coss was seven when the crash came, living with her aunt and uncle Geiger at Kenmare — already motherless, already given away to kin who would keep her.

chapter:seg-11-depression-and-dust:deep.spoken: 24 October 1929, Wall Street broke. North Dakota, already a farm state on the knife edge of weather, fell twice — prices for wheat and cattle collapsed while drought turned the western half of the country to powder. 1934 and 1936 were the cruelest years: grasses failed, cows starved, scorching winds piled dirt like snowdrifts, and grasshopper clouds ate what the wind left. More than a hundred twenty-one thousand people left the state; twelve thousand three hundred eight farms were foreclosed. Winifred Eloise Coss was seven when the crash came, living with her aunt and uncle Geiger at Kenmare — already motherless, already given away to kin who would keep her. 1930 census told the damage in rows. Coss, seventy-five, unemployed at 31 Euclid Avenue on Grand Forks' Red River floodplain; widower son Charles and grandsons Charles Jr. and Clarence crowded the house with firemen's wages. Next door, Ina Pearson kept roomers — a spelling that hinted at kinship never proved. Winfield Geiger owned a Kenmare service station and a thirty-four-hundred-dollar house; Lorraine raised Delores and seven-year-old niece Winifred with two young roomers.

It begins with the doors they would later walk through — kingdoms arguing over God, kings needing money, and a cold river in a country no Englishman yet called home. In 1485, on a Leicestershire field at Bosworth, the last Plantagenet king fell and a Welsh-born Tudor lifted a crown from the mud. Henry VII meant to end the Wars of the Roses; what he really did was hand his teenage son a restless island that would spend the next century tearing at its own faith. That son became Henry VIII in 1509 — eighteen years old, gifted, vain, and hungry for a male heir.

search:chapter-seg-01-doors-opening:deep.spoken: The story does not begin with our people. It begins with the doors they would later walk through — kingdoms arguing over God, kings needing money, and a cold river in a country no Englishman yet called home. In 1485, on a Leicestershire field at Bosworth, the last Plantagenet king fell and a Welsh-born Tudor lifted a crown from the mud. Henry VII meant to end the Wars of the Roses; what he really did was hand his teenage son a restless island that would spend the next century tearing at its own faith. That son became Henry VIII in 1509 — eighteen years old, gifted, vain, and hungry for a male heir. England was still Catholic then, still Latin in its liturgy, still paying its conscience to Rome. Within a generation all of that would crack. Across the Channel in 1517, a German monk named Martin Luther hammered his objections to indulgences onto a church door in Wittenberg. He had read the Latin Bible until the words burned in his hands. Rome ordered his books burned in 1521; by then the argument could not be unlit.

search:chapter-seg-02-two-doorways:brief.spoken: In 1575, while Elizabeth's court still balanced Catholic memory against Protestant law, a boy was born in Paris who would outlive every throne in this chapter. Louis Hébert — nine July 1575 — was not yet Canada's first farmer; he was simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells, in a kingdom that had its own wars of religion still to fight.

search:chapter-seg-02-two-doorways:stand.spoken: In 1575, while Elizabeth's court still balanced Catholic memory against Protestant law, a boy was born in Paris who would outlive every throne in this chapter. Louis Hébert — nine July 1575 — was not yet Canada's first farmer; he was simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells, in a kingdom that had its own wars of religion still to fight. Six years later and a hundred leagues north across the sea, another birth quietly mattered as much. On 25 March 1581, George Alcock came into the world at Cambridge in Cambridgeshire, son of Protestant minister John Richard Alcock and Alice Emily Abrich. No crown noted the day.

search:chapter-seg-02-two-doorways:deep.spoken: In 1575, while Elizabeth's court still balanced Catholic memory against Protestant law, a boy was born in Paris who would outlive every throne in this chapter. Louis Hébert — nine July 1575 — was not yet Canada's first farmer; he was simply a child in a city of stone bridges and church bells, in a kingdom that had its own wars of religion still to fight. Six years later and a hundred leagues north across the sea, another birth quietly mattered as much. On 25 March 1581, George Alcock came into the world at Cambridge in Cambridgeshire, son of Protestant minister John Richard Alcock and Alice Emily Abrich. No crown noted the day. No patent was issued. But the Powers branch of this tree — the English line that would one day meet the Hébert line in Iowa — starts here, in a university town smelling of river mud and ink. France was filling the St. Lawrence with names our cousins would carry. In Perche, Abraham Côsté was born in 1576; in La Rochelle, Jean Galleran Martin — called the Merchant of Metz — would father children who crossed the Atlantic.

search:chapter-seg-03-atlantic-crossings.spoken: The Atlantic in these years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, compasses that lied in fog, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares.

The Atlantic in these years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, compasses that lied in fog, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares. Our people were still mostly in parish registers on both shores, but the water between them was filling with names that would matter. In England the births came steady as rain. William Preston in Chesham in 1591.

The Atlantic in these years was not a bridge. It was a gamble — wood ships, salted pork, compasses that lied in fog, and kings who treated whole coastlines as chess squares. Our people were still mostly in parish registers on both shores, but the water between them was filling with names that would matter. In England the births came steady as rain. William Preston in Chesham in 1591. Edmund Rice in Berkhamsted in 1594. John Upham in Devon, John Bent in Hampshire, William Towne in Norfolk, Martha Blanchard and Arthur Gary — each a line on a page that would later cross the sea. On 6 January 1609, Thomas Barrett was born at Thornbury in Gloucestershire to Christopher Barrett and Elizabeth Clarke; his granddaughter would one day sit in a Boston jail accused of witchcraft. On 8 July 1605, Walter Powers Sr. was baptized at Stanton St.

search:event-evt-1613-140:brief.spoken: Note: From 1613 to 1620, the “Compagnie des Marchands“ was a monopoly Note: From 1613 to 1620, the “Compagnie des Marchands“ was a monopoly company supported by the French government that operated in Canada but failed to fulfill their contractual obligations and therefore lost their monopoly rights in 1621 to the “ Compagnie de Montmorency”.

search:chapter-seg-04-planting-towns:bri.spoken: After 1625 the story stops circling the Atlantic and starts putting down roots — wooden towns on Massachusetts mudflats, stone chimneys above the St. Lawrence ice, meetinghouses where freemen argued and rivers where beaver pelts bought muskets.

search:chapter-seg-04-planting-towns:sta.spoken: After 1625 the story stops circling the Atlantic and starts putting down roots — wooden towns on Massachusetts mudflats, stone chimneys above the St. Lawrence ice, meetinghouses where freemen argued and rivers where beaver pelts bought muskets. Two branches of one tree planted in the same generation, unaware of the Iowa wedding waiting at the far end. In 1626, while failed Gloucester fishermen retreated to Naumkeag and renamed it Salem without the king's permission, registers on both shores kept recording names. On 1 January, John Douglas Alcock was born at Markfield to George Alcock and Anne Hooker.

search:chapter-seg-04-planting-towns:dee.spoken: After 1625 the story stops circling the Atlantic and starts putting down roots — wooden towns on Massachusetts mudflats, stone chimneys above the St. Lawrence ice, meetinghouses where freemen argued and rivers where beaver pelts bought muskets. Two branches of one tree planted in the same generation, unaware of the Iowa wedding waiting at the far end. In 1626, while failed Gloucester fishermen retreated to Naumkeag and renamed it Salem without the king's permission, registers on both shores kept recording names. On 1 January, John Douglas Alcock was born at Markfield to George Alcock and Anne Hooker. Deacon John Upham married Elizabeth Webb at Bicton on 1 November. Robert Smith was born at Boston, Lincolnshire; Solomon Johnson married Eleanor Crofts in London. In France: Françoise Morin at Gahard, Anne Cloutier at Mortagne on 19 June, Pierre Tremblay at Randonnai, Pierre Saint-Denis and Vivienne Brunel at Dieppe and Rouen, Etienne Dumay Sr. at Dieppe, Sébastienne Veillon near La Rochelle, Marguerite Couillard at Québec on 6 August to Guillaume Couillard and Guillemette Hébert. Champlain granted Louis Hébert more land along the Saint-Charles River and opened the farm country at Château-Richer across from Île d'Orléans.

search:event-evt-1628-230:brief.spoken: In 1628, Robert Giffard with his new wife Marie Renouard sailed back In 1628, Robert Giffard with his new wife Marie Renouard sailed back to Canada to prepare for the immigrants they would soon be bringing from the Perche area of France. Their ship was also intercepted by English pirates and forced to return to France.

search:event-evt-1629-238:brief.spoken: On 9 Aug 1629, Samuel de Champlain formally surrendered his beloved Q On 9 Aug 1629, Samuel de Champlain formally surrendered his beloved Québec settlement to the English Kirke brothers. When the English came ashore to raise the English flag, they found the colony had only one tub of potatoes and roots left to feed the 67 people living in Colony.

search:event-evt-1633-270:brief.spoken: In 1633, England’s King Charles I appointed William Laud as the new A In 1633, England’s King Charles I appointed William Laud as the new Archbishop of Canterbury and head of the Anglian Church of England. search:event-evt-1633-271:brief.spoken: On 22 Mar 1633, Samuel de Champlain returned to the Québec settlement On 22 Mar 1633, Samuel de Champlain returned to the Québec settlement and began construction of a new settlement based on the framework of a traditional French “ville” (town) that was common in France at the time. The Québec settlement’s population was split into two groups.

search:event-evt-1634-284:brief.spoken: In 1634, thirty-year-old Françoise Garnier sailed with Robert Giffard In 1634, thirty-year-old Françoise Garnier sailed with Robert Giffard’s sponsored “Percheron Immigration” to the Québec settlement as the intended wife of Noël Langlois Sr. Françoise was one of the first of 262 filles à marier women.

search:event-evt-1636-308:brief.spoken: In Aug 1636, Jean Côté was granted a lot on the Grande-Allee, a boule In Aug 1636, Jean Côté was granted a lot on the Grande-Allee, a boulevard, which today is considered the “Champs Elysees” of Quebec City by the new Governor Montmagny of Quebec. (Champs Elysees is the main street in Paris France that runs under the Arc de Triomphe).

search:event-evt-1636-303:brief.spoken: Note: Private initiative and capital had always been involved in the Note: Private initiative and capital had always been involved in the operations of the British colonies, and the colonists were expected to provide for their own defense. By 1636, ten “Train Bands” (volunteer military companies) were formed in Boston and nearby towns.

event:evt-1636-303:brief.spoken: Note: Private initiative and capital had always been involved in the Note: Private initiative and capital had always been involved in the operations of the British colonies, and the colonists were expected to provide for their own defense. search:event-evt-1637-318:brief.spoken: In 1637, Nicholas Holt was one of ten men from the town of Newbury wh In 1637, Nicholas Holt was one of ten men from the town of Newbury who traveled 40 miles on foot to qualify for the “freeman oath” so they could vote for John Winthrop in the election for Governor of the Massachusetts Bay Colony to prevent Sir Henry Vanc from being re-elected.

search:chapter-seg-05-frontier-and-faith.spoken: After 1655 the margins grew teeth. English towns pushed inland toward groves already cleared by Pennacook hands; French farms clung to riverbanks while Iroquois warriors watched from every cedar swamp.

After 1655 the margins grew teeth. English towns pushed inland toward groves already cleared by Pennacook hands; French farms clung to riverbanks while Iroquois warriors watched from every cedar swamp. Faith meant different things on each shore — Puritan meetinghouses where Eliot's Praying Indians wore English coats, and stone chapels where Jesuits exorcised devils from teenage girls. Both branches were about to learn that frontier peace is always borrowed time. 1656–1658 kept the registers busy on both sides of the ocean.

After 1655 the margins grew teeth. English towns pushed inland toward groves already cleared by Pennacook hands; French farms clung to riverbanks while Iroquois warriors watched from every cedar swamp. Faith meant different things on each shore — Puritan meetinghouses where Eliot's Praying Indians wore English coats, and stone chapels where Jesuits exorcised devils from teenage girls. Both branches were about to learn that frontier peace is always borrowed time. 1656–1658 kept the registers busy on both sides of the ocean. Edward Peter Hurst died in Boston; his daughter Margaret had married Edward Preston at New Haven in 1651. William Towne sold Salem land and bought Topsfield meadow. Robert Smith, tailor of Rowley, married Mary French. Eleven-year-old William Taylor came alone on the Speedwell toward country that would become Marlborough. In Canada, Robert Drouin's prosperity showed in dowries — from nothing in 1656 to milk cows, clothing, and livres cash within eight years.

search:event-evt-1670-586:brief.spoken: On 2 May 1670, the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) was chartered making On 2 May 1670, the “Hudson’s Bay Company” (HBC) was chartered making it the oldest incorporated joint-stock merchandising company in the English-speaking world. search:chapter-seg-06-wars-and-witch-pan.spoken: The war did not end when the smoke cleared. It changed shape — from burning garrisons to burning charters, from Iroquois canoes on the Richelieu to English cannon on the St.

The war did not end when the smoke cleared. It changed shape — from burning garrisons to burning charters, from Iroquois canoes on the Richelieu to English cannon on the St. Lawrence, from frontier militia to courtroom spectral evidence. Between 1676 and 1692 both branches learned that peace treaties and prayer books are written on paper the powerful can rewrite overnight. King Philip's War bled out through 1676.

The war did not end when the smoke cleared. It changed shape — from burning garrisons to burning charters, from Iroquois canoes on the Richelieu to English cannon on the St. Lawrence, from frontier militia to courtroom spectral evidence. Between 1676 and 1692 both branches learned that peace treaties and prayer books are written on paper the powerful can rewrite overnight. King Philip's War bled out through 1676. At the Great Swamp Fight sixty-eight colonists died and a hundred fifty were wounded; twenty-two more perished on the march home. Phineas Upham, carried wounded to Wickford, then dragged through the "Hungry March" where soldiers ate their own horses — died of his wounds at Malden, date unknown but autumn 1676. Groton burned 13 March; Richard Sawtelle fled thirty miles east to Watertown with his family, leaving one of five garrison houses in ashes. Widower William Taylor married widow Hannah Merriam 16 July 1677 — her first husband Henry Axtell killed on the Marlborough-Sudbury road in April 1676; she brought five children. Eliezer Taylor born April 1678 was named for Deacon William Ward's son slain in the war.

search:chapter-seg-07-british-north-amer.spoken: After the witch panic, the colonies did not rest. They multiplied — towns west of Boston, farms on stolen Praying land, stone forts on the Richelieu, trading posts on lakes no European map had named.

After the witch panic, the colonies did not rest. They multiplied — towns west of Boston, farms on stolen Praying land, stone forts on the Richelieu, trading posts on lakes no European map had named. Between 1693 and 1750 the English branch learned to live as subjects of a crown, not shareholders in a company; the French branch learned that peace treaties with thirty-nine nations could hold sixteen years and still leave the border smoking. One million English souls against sixty thousand French by mid-century — yet Canada covered more ground than the thirteen colonies, and every river between them was a future battlefield. The Powers line took root where Nashoba had been.

After the witch panic, the colonies did not rest. They multiplied — towns west of Boston, farms on stolen Praying land, stone forts on the Richelieu, trading posts on lakes no European map had named. Between 1693 and 1750 the English branch learned to live as subjects of a crown, not shareholders in a company; the French branch learned that peace treaties with thirty-nine nations could hold sixteen years and still leave the border smoking. One million English souls against sixty thousand French by mid-century — yet Canada covered more ground than the thirteen colonies, and every river between them was a future battlefield. The Powers line took root where Nashoba had been. 16 December 1696, Walter Powers III married Rebecca Barrett at Littleton — granddaughter of Chelmsford Barretts, daughter of Joseph and Martha Gould — and kept an inn on the northerly part of town near Brown Hill and Spectacle Pond. When elder brother William died, Walter inherited their father Walter Jr.'s Concord house; by 1704 he was caring for sixty-five-year-old Walter Jr. and sixty-three-year-old Tryall at Nashoba. 22 September 1710, Josiah Powers Sr. was born in a garrison house on Neshoba Hill — seventh and last child of Walter and Rebecca, the name that would reach Vermont and Iowa.

search:chapter-seg-08-empires-at-war:bri.spoken: The final French and Indian War was not a footnote — it was the hinge. Sixty thousand French subjects against two million British colonists, yet Canada held the interior until the ships and muskets tipped.

search:chapter-seg-08-empires-at-war:sta.spoken: The final French and Indian War was not a footnote — it was the hinge. Sixty thousand French subjects against two million British colonists, yet Canada held the interior until the ships and muskets tipped. When the smoke cleared in 1763, New France was British, thirteen colonies were angry, and two branches of one family stood on opposite sides of a border that would not hold peace for twenty years. Between 1751 and 1815 the English line learned revolution; the French line learned to live under a Protestant king and keep their language; and a Cree métis girl on the Red River would one day meet a Vermont soldier's grandson in a way no parish register in Perche could have predicted. 28 May 1754: twenty-two-year-old Lt.

search:chapter-seg-08-empires-at-war:dee.spoken: The final French and Indian War was not a footnote — it was the hinge. Sixty thousand French subjects against two million British colonists, yet Canada held the interior until the ships and muskets tipped. When the smoke cleared in 1763, New France was British, thirteen colonies were angry, and two branches of one family stood on opposite sides of a border that would not hold peace for twenty years. Between 1751 and 1815 the English line learned revolution; the French line learned to live under a Protestant king and keep their language; and a Cree métis girl on the Red River would one day meet a Vermont soldier's grandson in a way no parish register in Perche could have predicted. 28 May 1754: twenty-two-year-old Lt. Colonel George Washington ambushed a French patrol at Jumonville Glen — the Forks of the Ohio, Fort Duquesne, Pittsburgh not yet invented. The fourth French and Indian War exploded across the continent and the Atlantic; Britain blockaded Canada while Acadians who refused the oath were scattered — some toward Louisiana, seed of the Cajun name. Seven Years' War followed in Europe 1756. Montcalm took Fort William Henry 1757; Native allies massacred surrendered British troops and carried smallpox back to the Ohio valley. Louisbourg fell 1758.

search:chapter-seg-09-west-to-iowa:brief.spoken: Peace did not mean stillness. The Treaty of Ghent had ended the shooting, but the continent kept rearranging itself — borders surveyed straight because rivers were too quarrelsome, fur companies merged because competition bled profit, and young people who had never seen Perche or Cambridge started walking toward land no parish had named.

search:chapter-seg-09-west-to-iowa:stand.spoken: Peace did not mean stillness. The Treaty of Ghent had ended the shooting, but the continent kept rearranging itself — borders surveyed straight because rivers were too quarrelsome, fur companies merged because competition bled profit, and young people who had never seen Perche or Cambridge started walking toward land no parish had named. Between 1816 and 1853 the English branch learned canals and railroad timetables; the French branch learned to cross borders without papers and change a surname to English; and Iowa, which had been someone else's cornfield yesterday, became the place where Edith Powers' grandson would marry a métis girl's daughter and finally stitch Winifred's tree. 20 October 1818: the United States and Britain signed the Convention of 1818 — fisheries, slave restoration clauses, joint occupation of Oregon, and a boundary at the 49th parallel because a straight line surveys easier than a watershed. Britain ceded Rupert's Land south of the line, including the Red River Colony below the parallel; the United States gave up the northern edge of Missouri Territory.

search:chapter-seg-09-west-to-iowa:deep.spoken: Peace did not mean stillness. The Treaty of Ghent had ended the shooting, but the continent kept rearranging itself — borders surveyed straight because rivers were too quarrelsome, fur companies merged because competition bled profit, and young people who had never seen Perche or Cambridge started walking toward land no parish had named. Between 1816 and 1853 the English branch learned canals and railroad timetables; the French branch learned to cross borders without papers and change a surname to English; and Iowa, which had been someone else's cornfield yesterday, became the place where Edith Powers' grandson would marry a métis girl's daughter and finally stitch Winifred's tree. 20 October 1818: the United States and Britain signed the Convention of 1818 — fisheries, slave restoration clauses, joint occupation of Oregon, and a boundary at the 49th parallel because a straight line surveys easier than a watershed. Britain ceded Rupert's Land south of the line, including the Red River Colony below the parallel; the United States gave up the northern edge of Missouri Territory. French missionaries at Red River worked with Ojibwe and Cree families; by 1823 roughly eight hundred métis households lived in the colony and present-day North Dakota. In 1821 Parliament forced the North West Company into the Hudson's Bay Company — André Carrière's employer changed names but not latitude. 1820 census: Nathaniel Powers at Westminster, Vermont, with Esther and seven children including twenty-year-old Edith; widow Elizabeth Johnson Smith at Phillipston; Warren Coss, twenty-nine, in Lowell but missing from the enumerator's book. 30 September 1824, Warren Coss married Edith Powers at Lowell, Middlesex County — the short Coss stub grafted onto the long Powers limb Nathaniel and Esther had grown in Vermont hill towns. July 1827, Joseph Warren Coss was born to Warren and Edith; no birth certificate survives and census pages disagree between Massachusetts and New Hampshire, but Martha Coss, one year old, died at Peterborough two months later — the grave argues for Hillsborough County.

search:chapter-seg-10-dakota-years:brief.spoken: The wedding in Winneshiek County was not an ending — it was a departure. Between 1854 and 1928 the merged family learned what it meant to hold land with a filing fee and a prayer, to lose a wife at thirty-nine on a Red River floodplain, and to raise children within earshot of tepees and buffalo hunts.

search:chapter-seg-10-dakota-years:stand.spoken: The wedding in Winneshiek County was not an ending — it was a departure. Between 1854 and 1928 the merged family learned what it meant to hold land with a filing fee and a prayer, to lose a wife at thirty-nine on a Red River floodplain, and to raise children within earshot of tepees and buffalo hunts. Homestead patents, Civil War pensions, stagecoach miles, and a hotel ledger at Grand Forks carried the story from Iowa corn to Dakota wheat while cousins in Ontario fished Lake Huron and a métis great-grandfather's name still appeared on a St. Boniface map. 1854 poured settlers into Iowa — ferries over the Mississippi running day and night, canvas tents blooming on open ground.

search:chapter-seg-10-dakota-years:deep.spoken: The wedding in Winneshiek County was not an ending — it was a departure. Between 1854 and 1928 the merged family learned what it meant to hold land with a filing fee and a prayer, to lose a wife at thirty-nine on a Red River floodplain, and to raise children within earshot of tepees and buffalo hunts. Homestead patents, Civil War pensions, stagecoach miles, and a hotel ledger at Grand Forks carried the story from Iowa corn to Dakota wheat while cousins in Ontario fished Lake Huron and a métis great-grandfather's name still appeared on a St. Boniface map. 1854 poured settlers into Iowa — ferries over the Mississippi running day and night, canvas tents blooming on open ground. 29 March, Benjamin Goodwater III naturalized at Winneshiek and homesteaded Frankville, twenty miles from Minnesota; Québec abolished the seigneurial system he had already left behind. Henry Raymond and Elizabeth Falconer married at Saugeen, Bruce County, Ontario — no register found. 8 November 1855, Edgar Putnam Coss was born at Sauk Centre, Wisconsin, two hundred sixty miles northwest of his parents' Iowa wedding. 1860 census: Warren Coss machinist at Hebron, Jefferson County, with Edith, twins Helen and Henry, Whitcomb, and young Goodwater nephews in the house; Joseph and Mary Ann farmed Cherry Grove, Minnesota, beside Norman Goodwater; Benjamin held twelve hundred dollars of Iowa soil at Glenwood. Minnesota statehood 1858 left unorganized land the Sioux had not surrendered; Congress created Dakota Territory 2 March 1861.

search:event-evt-1854-889:brief.spoken: In 1854, the old French feudal seigneurial system in Québec Lower Can In 1854, the old French feudal seigneurial system in Québec Lower Canada was finally officially abolished, after Benjamin Goodwater III and Mary Louise (Carriere) and their children had left Québec seeking land and opportunity in the United States.

search:chapter-seg-11-depression-and-dus.spoken: 24 October 1929, Wall Street broke. North Dakota, already a farm state on the knife edge of weather, fell twice — prices for wheat and cattle collapsed while drought turned the western half of the country to powder.

24 October 1929, Wall Street broke. North Dakota, already a farm state on the knife edge of weather, fell twice — prices for wheat and cattle collapsed while drought turned the western half of the country to powder. 1934 and 1936 were the cruelest years: grasses failed, cows starved, scorching winds piled dirt like snowdrifts, and grasshopper clouds ate what the wind left. More than a hundred twenty-one thousand people left the state; twelve thousand three hundred eight farms were foreclosed. Winifred Eloise Coss was seven when the crash came, living with her aunt and uncle Geiger at Kenmare — already motherless, already given away to kin who would keep her.

24 October 1929, Wall Street broke. North Dakota, already a farm state on the knife edge of weather, fell twice — prices for wheat and cattle collapsed while drought turned the western half of the country to powder. 1934 and 1936 were the cruelest years: grasses failed, cows starved, scorching winds piled dirt like snowdrifts, and grasshopper clouds ate what the wind left. More than a hundred twenty-one thousand people left the state; twelve thousand three hundred eight farms were foreclosed. Winifred Eloise Coss was seven when the crash came, living with her aunt and uncle Geiger at Kenmare — already motherless, already given away to kin who would keep her. 1930 census told the damage in rows. Coss, seventy-five, unemployed at 31 Euclid Avenue on Grand Forks' Red River floodplain; widower son Charles and grandsons Charles Jr. and Clarence crowded the house with firemen's wages. Next door, Ina Pearson kept roomers — a spelling that hinted at kinship never proved. Winfield Geiger owned a Kenmare service station and a thirty-four-hundred-dollar house; Lorraine raised Delores and seven-year-old niece Winifred with two young roomers.
